title: The bouquet pattern, stylus work on a nonpareil base
subtitle: How to produce the floral motif from a completed nonpareil
excerpt: The bouquet (also called the feather or peacock pattern) is produced by drawing a fine stylus in a series of tight S-curves through a completed nonpareil, pulling alternate stripe intersections into opposing arcs. The result is a repeating flower-like motif that was among the most fashionable of the 18th-century marbled bindings.
sourceNotes: Josef Halfer, The Progress of the Marbling Art (1893, English translation 1894): Halfer describes the bouquet as a natural development from the nonpareil, requiring the same base but a second tool pass with a stylus moving in opposed arcs. C. W. Woolnough, The Art of Marbling (1881) also describes the feather pattern family.

glossaryTerms.definition: The completed fine-stripe zigzag pattern produced by two comb passes before the stylus work begins. The bouquet cannot be worked without a tight nonpareil first.
The stylus motion that produces the bouquet: the tip is drawn through the nonpareil in alternating left and right arcs, pulling stripes into opposed curves at each crossing.

content.text: The bouquet pattern begins with a fully completed

. The stylus pass is done immediately after the nonpareil is completed, before the pigment has time to settle. Speed and the width of each arc determine how tight or open the resulting flower motif becomes.

attrs.title: Marbling chemistry safety
attrs.body: Alum (potassium aluminium sulphate) is a mild skin and eye irritant. Wear gloves when mixing or applying it to paper and rinse splashes promptly. Carrageenan is non-toxic but spilled size is very slippery, wipe spills immediately. Do not eat or drink at the marbling tray.

items.name: Carrageenan (marbling size)
items.notes: Prepared 24 hours ahead at 1.5-2%. Surface must be clear of air bubbles.

items.name: Marbling acrylic, liquid
items.notes: Four or five colours tested for spread. More colours produce a denser bouquet.

items.name: Ox-gall (marbling)
items.notes: Adjusted for each colour to produce an even spread on the size surface.

items.name: Alum, potash (mordant)
items.notes: Applied to paper and fully dried. A damp alum coat will cause the sheet to pick up the pattern unevenly.

items.name: Marbling tray
items.notes: At least the size of the sheet.

items.name: Ebru comb set
items.notes: Both the wide-tooth rake and the fine-tooth comb are needed to build the nonpareil base.

items.name: Marbling stylus
items.notes: The stylus tip draws the bouquet curves. A sharpened wooden skewer works; a dedicated marbling stylus is easier to control.

content.text: Drop and distribute pigments across the size surface, then complete both the wide-tooth rake pass and the fine-tooth comb passes to produce the
. The nonpareil must be tight and even before the stylus work begins, a loose or uneven nonpareil produces a muddled bouquet.

Position the stylus at the top left of the tray, resting its tip just on the size surface. Draw it downward in a shallow left arc, then swing immediately to a right arc, then left again, continuing to the bottom of the tray. This

is repeated across the full tray width, spacing each pass at roughly the same interval as the nonpareil tooth spacing. The arc width determines the size of the finished bouquet motif, wider arcs produce a larger, more open flower; tighter arcs produce a dense, compact pattern.

Hold the alum-treated sheet by its two short edges and lower it onto the size centre-first, releasing both edges simultaneously. Allow ten to fifteen seconds of contact, then peel one long edge back in a smooth motion. Rinse immediately under running water and lay flat to dry.

items.problem: Bouquet motifs are muddy rather than clean
items.solution: The nonpareil base was too loose or the stylus pass was made too slowly, giving pigment time to merge. Tighten the nonpareil tooth spacing and increase the pace of the S-curve pass.

items.problem: Arcs are uneven in size across the sheet
items.solution: The stylus tip depth in the size varied during the pass. Keep the tip just touching the surface throughout.

items.problem: Pattern disappears when paper is rinsed
items.solution: The alum coat was not fully dry before laying the sheet. Apply alum at least 30 minutes before use and allow to air-dry completely.
